\needspace{4\baselineskip}
\section{Pasta Carbonara}
\recipemeta{4 servings · 10 minutes prep · 20 minutes cook}
\recipedescription{A rich, creamy Roman classic made the authentic way—no cream needed.}
\subsection*{Ingredients}
\begin{ingredients}
\item \textbf{water} -- 4 liters
\item \textbf{salt} -- 2 tbsp
\item \textbf{guanciale} -- 200 g
\item \textbf{pancetta} -- 200 g
\item \textbf{eggs} -- 3
\item \textbf{egg yolks} -- 2
\item \textbf{pecorino romano} -- 100 g, finely grated
\item \textbf{black pepper} -- generous amount, freshly ground
\item \textbf{spaghetti} -- 400 g
\item \textbf{pasta water} -- 1 cup
\end{ingredients}
\subsection*{Instructions}
\begin{enumerate}
\item Bring a large pot of water to boil. Season generously with salt.
\item While waiting, cut guanciale into small strips. If unavailable, use pancetta instead.
\item Whisk together eggs, egg yolks, and pecorino romano in a mixing bowl. Season with black pepper.
\item Cook spaghetti in the boiling water for 2 minutes less than package directions.
\item Meanwhile, cook the guanciale in a cold skillet over medium heat for 8 minutes until fat renders and meat is crispy.
\item Reserve pasta water, then drain pasta. Off heat, add pasta to skillet, then quickly stir in egg mixture. Toss vigorously, adding pasta water as needed to create a creamy sauce.
\item Serve immediately with additional pecorino and black pepper.
\end{enumerate}
\recipeattribution{Recipe by J. Kenji López-Alt, adapted by PJ}
\vspace{3\baselineskip}
